title: Marvell Crashed the S&P 500 Today. Jensen Huang Says It's a Trillion-Dollar Company.
subtitle: Custom ASICs, hyperscaler partnerships, and a $2B Nvidia bet are turning this former also-ran into the next semiconductor heavyweight — and Wall Street is still catching up.
summary: Marvell just joined the S&P 500. Its stock tripled in 2026. Jensen Huang called it the next trillion-dollar chipmaker. Here's why custom ASICs and a $2B Nvidia bet are just the beginning.

image.caption: Professional stock photograph — extreme close-up macro shot of a silicon wafer with intricate circuit patterns, illuminated by blue and copper data center server lights. AI-generated via FAL.ai.

bodyHtml:
Today — June 22, 2026 — Marvell Technology joined the S&P 500. That'd be a story. It's not the headline.

The headline is Jensen Huang at Computex calling Marvell "the next trillion-dollar semiconductor company." The headline is a stock that tripled this year — up 247.87% from $89 to $311 — while Wall Street still hasn't priced in what's happening. Every hyperscaler needs custom silicon, and Marvell can deliver.

The committee finally noticed. The real ones saw it coming months ago.

The Custom Silicon Moat

Here's what most people miss: Nvidia sells shovels, but the biggest gold miners are tired of paying retail. Amazon, Microsoft, Google — all designing their own chips. Custom ASICs. Purpose-built. Cheaper per inference at scale.

Marvell is the #2 custom ASIC designer behind Broadcom. Revenue hit $8.2 billion this fiscal year, growing 27.6% YoY. Next year's outlook: $11.5 billion — a 40% jump. Gross margins: 51.5%. Real silicon. Real data centers. Real money.

Amazon. Microsoft. Nvidia. Repeat.

Amazon's Trainium — the custom AI chip powering AWS? Marvell designed it. And AWS may sell Trainium externally, blowing the TAM wide open. Microsoft's Maia accelerator for Azure? Marvell is in the room. Microsoft doesn't hand out silicon roadmap invites casually.

Then there's Nvidia — not a customer, a partner. Nvidia invested $2 billion in Marvell and co-developed NVLink Fusion, integrating Marvell tech directly into the Nvidia data center ecosystem. The company that dominates AI chips isn't competing with Marvell. They're collaborating. That's a klaxon, not a signal.

▶ The $2 Billion Bet — NVIDIA x Marvell at COMPUTEX 2026
NVIDIA x Marvell: The Big New Partnership Powering AI — TaiwanPlus Docs

Jensen Doesn't Say Things He Doesn't Mean

At Computex, Jensen Huang called Marvell "the next trillion-dollar semiconductor company." He runs the most valuable company on Earth. He doesn't do empty flattery. When he name-checks a partner like that, the sell-side should listen.

NVLink Fusion means Marvell's interconnects are woven into Nvidia's data center architecture. That's not a supplier. That's integration. And it's not just silicon — Marvell's optical interconnect business is growing ~70%, replacing copper in data centers. They acquired Celestial AI for $3.25 billion and Polariton Technologies. June brought the Teralynx T100: a 102.4 Tbps switch, industry-first for AI and cloud. This isn't a chip company anymore — it's infrastructure wearing a chip company's clothes.

Yeah, But...

Trailing P/E: 106x. Forward: 50x. Priced for perfection. Debt sits at $5.28 billion against $3.84 billion cash — manageable at 40% growth, ugly if the cycle turns.

The real risk: AIchip may take the lead on future Trainium iterations. If Amazon rotates its design partner, Marvell loses its crown jewel hyperscaler program. That's not theoretical.

The counter: 41 analysts at Strong Buy. High target: $385 — 24% upside from here. Those targets were set before S&P 500 inclusion and before Jensen's Computex remarks fully circulated. The numbers may already be stale.

The Bottom Line

Marvell isn't Nvidia. It doesn't need to be. The custom ASIC market is big enough for multiple winners, and Marvell holds the #2 position behind Broadcom with Amazon, Microsoft, and Nvidia as simultaneous partners. Nobody else can say that.

S&P 500 inclusion today forces passive funds to buy. Active managers who slept on this name will have to explain missing a stock that tripled in six months. If Jensen is right about that trillion-dollar market cap — do the math from $271.9 billion.

The train left at $89. It hasn't reached the terminal yet.

— The Signal
